text.title: DDD Repositories Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules grounding the DDD Repository pattern.

rule.statement: The Repository interface MUST live in the Domain layer and MUST NOT import any ORM/infrastructure type (no SQLAlchemy, no Django ORM, no JPA/Hibernate, no EF Core). It declares methods using only domain types: `find_by_id(UUID) -> Optional[Aggregate]`, `save(Aggregate) -> None`, `delete(Aggregate) -> None`.
rule.rationale: Domain-owned port keeps the domain testable in isolation; tests substitute an in-memory repository without spinning up any persistence layer.

rule.statement: The Repository MUST return fully reconstituted Aggregate objects, never ORM model instances. Infrastructure implementation translates `_to_entity()` / `_to_model()` through a mapper.
rule.rationale: Returning ORM models leaks persistence shape into domain code; lazy-loaded relations become hidden runtime dependencies.

rule.statement: Repositories MUST expose only identity-based lookups (`find_by_id`, `find_by_external_key`). Arbitrary-criteria queries (`find_orders_for_customer_in_date_range`) MUST live in a separate Read Model / Query Service, not in the repository interface.

rationale.source: CQRS + Vaughn Vernon — Effective Aggregate Design

rule.rationale: Mixing identity load + arbitrary query inflates the interface and tempts callers to bypass the aggregate boundary.

rule.statement: The Infrastructure implementation MUST own the persistence ↔ domain mapping. It deserializes ORM rows into aggregates and serializes aggregate state back. Domain code MUST NOT contain any ORM-aware field.
rule.rationale: The adapter contains all framework knowledge; swapping ORMs touches only this file.

rule.statement: Tests MUST be able to substitute an in-memory repository implementing the same interface. The repository interface is a port; SQL/Mongo implementations are adapters.
rule.rationale: Without a swappable port, domain tests pay the cost of spinning up a database; testing speed degrades and CI flakiness rises.
